items.correct: Bundling data and methods together while restricting direct access to internal state.
items.random_wrong: Encrypting data so it cannot be read.
items.why: Students take 'capsule' too literally. Encapsulation combines two ideas: keeping data and behaviour TOGETHER, and HIDING implementation behind a public interface.

items.q: Why must a recursive function have a base case?
items.correct: Without it, the function calls itself indefinitely, causing a stack overflow.
items.random_wrong: To make the function run faster.
items.why: Students treat the base case as optional ceremony. It's the TERMINATION CONDITION that stops recursion - each call adds a stack frame, and without termination the stack will overflow.

items.q: What happens when 11111111 + 1 is computed in an 8-bit register?
items.correct: Overflow - the result requires 9 bits but only 8 are available.
items.why: Students miss that registers have fixed width. The mathematical result is 9 bits, but the 8-bit register drops the carry-out, producing 00000000 with an overflow flag.
Correct answer: one call, multiple implementations. “only one class” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: encapsulation. “inheritance” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
